approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(V! * E)
approaches.summary: Explore all possible paths from source to destination using depth-first search, keeping track of the shortest path found. This is impractical for all but the smallest graphs due to exponential time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DFS for Shortest Path with Path Reconstruction
ladders.desc: Find the shortest path from source to destination by exploring all possible paths using DFS and reconstructing the optimal path.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the shortest path problem by exhaustively exploring every possible path from source to destination using depth-first search. The intuition is that if we check every possible route, we're guaranteed to find the shortest one. However, this comes at an enormous computational cost of O(V! * E), making it impractical for all but the smallest graphs. The algorithm works by maintaining a current path and distance as we traverse the graph. When we reach the destination, we compare the current distance with the best found so far. We also keep track of the actual path sequence to reconstruct the optimal route. The key insight is that we must mark nodes as visited during each recursive call to avoid cycles, then unmark them when backtracking to explore alternative paths. This brute force method is conceptually simple but computationally infeasible for real-world problems, serving mainly as a baseline to understand why more efficient algorithms like Dijkstra are necessary. The space complexity is O(V) for the recursion stack and path storage. Common mistakes include forgetting to unmark visited nodes when backtracking (which would prevent exploring all paths), not handling disconnected graphs properly, and inefficient path reconstruction. The connection to practical applications is limited due to the factorial time complexity, but understanding this approach helps appreciate the need for optimization in graph algorithms.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Path Exploration with Backtracking
ladders.desc: Depth-first search that explores all paths from current node to destination while tracking distance and path.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach—a recursive DFS function that systematically explores every possible path from the current node to the destination. The intuition is that by recursively visiting each neighbor and backtracking, we can enumerate all simple paths (paths without cycles) in the graph. The algorithm takes the current node, current distance, current path, visited set, and maintains global best distance and best path. For each unvisited neighbor, it adds the edge weight to the distance, appends the neighbor to the path, marks it visited, and recurses. When the destination is reached, it compares the current distance with the best found. After exploring all neighbors, it backtracks by removing the node from the path and marking it unvisited. The time complexity for this sub-routine in isolation is O(V! * E) in worst case because it explores all permutations of nodes. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion stack and path storage. This feeds into the full problem by providing the exhaustive search mechanism. Common mistakes include not unmarking visited nodes during backtracking (which would miss paths), incorrect distance accumulation, and not handling base cases properly. The real code pattern involves careful state management during recursion.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Exhaustive Path Enumeration with Cycle Avoidance
ladders.desc: Systematically generate all simple paths from source to destination while avoiding cycles.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on the mechanism of enumerating all possible simple paths (paths without repeating nodes) between two nodes in a graph. The intuition is that to find the shortest path by brute force, we must consider every possible route, but we must avoid cycles because they would create infinite loops and never reach the destination. The algorithm uses a visited set to track which nodes are in the current path. When moving to a neighbor, we check if it's already visited; if so, we skip it to prevent cycles. This ensures we only explore simple paths. The step-by-step process: start with source node in path and visited set, for each unvisited neighbor, add to path and visited, recurse, then remove from path and visited when backtracking. The time complexity is factorial because in a complete graph, the number of simple paths grows factorially with nodes. Space complexity is O(V) for the visited set and path. This feeds into the DFS sub-routine by providing the cycle-avoidance mechanism. Common mistakes include using a global visited set instead of per-path visited (which would incorrectly prevent exploring alternative paths), and not properly managing the visited set during recursion. The real code pattern involves passing visited as a parameter that's modified during recursion and restored during backtracking.

From 0, neighbor 1: path=[0,1], visited={0,1}.
From 1, neighbor 2: path=[0,1,2], reached destination, record path.
From 1, neighbor 0: 0 is in visited, skip to avoid cycle.
Backtrack from 1: remove 1, visited={0}.
No more neighbors from 0. Total paths enumerated: 1 simple path.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal with State Tracking
ladders.desc: Traverse a graph while maintaining current path and visited nodes.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves traversing a graph (like DFS or BFS) while keeping track of the current state—specifically the sequence of nodes visited so far (the path) and which nodes have been visited to avoid reprocessing. The intuition is that many graph algorithms need to remember where they've been and how they got there. For brute force shortest path, we need the path to compute distance and reconstruct the route, and the visited set to avoid cycles. The algorithm starts at a source node with an empty path and visited set. As we move to neighbors, we append the node to the path and add it to visited. When we backtrack, we remove the node from the path and visited. This state tracking is crucial for enumerating all simple paths. Time complexity depends on traversal type; for DFS it's O(V + E) for a single traversal, but in our context it's part of a larger factorial process. Space complexity is O(V) for path and visited storage. This feeds into exhaustive enumeration by providing the mechanism to track progress. Common mistakes include not copying the visited set when passing to recursion (leading to shared state errors), and incorrect order of adding/removing from path. The real code pattern uses lists for paths and sets for visited, with careful push/pop operations.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Recursive Graph Node Visiting
ladders.desc: Visit a graph node recursively, exploring its neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is the fundamental recursive step of graph traversal: given a current node, visit it, then recursively visit each of its unvisited neighbors. The intuition is that recursion naturally fits graph traversal because each node leads to multiple neighbors, creating a tree-like exploration. The algorithm: define a function visit(node) that marks node as visited, then for each neighbor not visited, call visit(neighbor). This is the essence of depth-first search. In pseudocode: function visit(node): visited.add(node); for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor not in visited: visit(neighbor). Time complexity is O(V + E) for visiting all nodes and edges once. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion stack and visited set. This feeds into state tracking by providing the traversal mechanism. Common mistakes include infinite recursion in cyclic graphs (solved by visited set), stack overflow for deep graphs, and not handling disconnected graphs. The real code pattern is straightforward recursion with a base case implied by visited checks.

Neighbors of 0: [1]. 1 not visited, call visit(1).
In visit(1): mark 1 visited.
Neighbors of 1: [2]. 2 not visited, call visit(2).
In visit(2): mark 2 visited.
Neighbors of 2: none, return.
Return to visit(1), return to visit(0). Done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation and Node Neighbors
ladders.desc: Understand how graphs are represented and how to access neighbors of a node.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers the basics of graph representation, which is essential for any graph algorithm. A graph consists of nodes (vertices) and edges connecting them. For shortest path problems, edges often have weights. The two most common representations are adjacency list and adjacency matrix. In adjacency list, each node has a list of its neighbors, often stored as a dictionary or array of lists. For weighted graphs, each entry includes the neighbor and weight. Accessing neighbors means retrieving this list for a given node. The intuition is that to traverse a graph, we need to know which nodes are connected. For example, if graph[0] = [(1,2), (2,3)], it means node 0 has an edge to node 1 with weight 2, and to node 2 with weight 3. This is the building block for all graph traversals. Time complexity to access neighbors is O(1) on average for adjacency list. Space complexity is O(V + E) for adjacency list. This feeds into recursive visiting by providing the neighbor information. Common mistakes include incorrect graph representation (e.g., forgetting weights), assuming undirected vs directed graphs, and off-by-one errors in node indexing. The real code pattern is simple data structure access.

approaches.name: Dijkstra with Predecessor Array O((V + E) log V)
approaches.summary: Use Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap to find shortest distances, and maintain a predecessor array to reconstruct the path by backtracking from the destination. This is the standard approach for non-negative edge weights.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Dijkstra with Path Reconstruction
ladders.desc: Find shortest path distances from source to all nodes and reconstruct the actual shortest path to a target node using Dijkstra's algorithm with predecessor tracking.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the shortest path distances from a source node to all other nodes in a weighted graph with non-negative edges, and reconstructing the actual shortest path to a specific target node. The standard approach uses Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap for efficiency, combined with a predecessor array to track the path. The intuition is that Dijkstra's algorithm greedily expands the shortest known distance frontier, and by storing which node we came from when we update a distance, we can reconstruct the path by backtracking from the destination. The algorithm initializes all distances to infinity except the source (0), uses a min-heap to process nodes in order of increasing distance, relaxes edges to update distances and predecessors, and finally reconstructs the path by following predecessors from the target back to the source. Time complexity is O((V + E) log V) due to heap operations for each edge relaxation, and space is O(V) for distances, predecessors, and heap. This feeds into practical applications like GPS navigation. Common mistakes include not handling unreachable nodes (distance remains infinity), forgetting to check for negative weights (Dijkstra fails), and incorrect path reconstruction when multiple shortest paths exist (algorithm finds one). Real code pattern: Use heapq for min-heap, distances dict/list, predecessors dict/list, and backtracking list.

Heap pop (3,1): process node 1: relax edge to 3 (dist 3+1=4 < 6, update dist[3]=4, pred[3]=1, heap push (4,3)). Heap pop (4,3): process node 3 (target reached). Reconstruct path: start at 3, pred[3]=1, pred[1]=2, pred[2]=0, reverse to get [0,2,1,3]. Distance to 3 is 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Dijkstra's Algorithm with Distance Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement Dijkstra's algorithm to compute shortest distances from a source to all nodes using a min-heap, without path reconstruction.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the core Dijkstra's algorithm to find shortest distances from a source node to all other nodes in a graph with non-negative edge weights, using a min-heap for efficiency. The intuition is to maintain a set of nodes whose shortest distance is known, starting with the source, and repeatedly expand the node with the smallest tentative distance, updating neighbors' distances if a shorter path is found. This works because with non-negative weights, the smallest tentative distance is guaranteed to be the final shortest distance. The algorithm initializes all distances to infinity except the source (0), uses a min-heap to store (distance, node) pairs, processes nodes in increasing distance order, and relaxes edges to update distances. Time complexity is O((V + E) log V) as each edge may cause a heap push/pop, and space is O(V) for distances and heap. This feeds into the full problem by providing the distance array needed for path reconstruction. Common mistakes include not using a heap (leading to O(V^2) time), not marking nodes as processed (can cause re-processing), and incorrect initialization. Real code pattern: Use heapq, distances list, visited set optional (since heap ensures processing), and relaxation loop.

Pop (2,1): relax 1->2 (2+1=3 < 5, update dist[2]=3, push (3,2)). Pop (3,2): no new relaxations. Final distances: [0,2,3].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Edge Relaxation with Predecessor Update
ladders.desc: Given current distances and a node, update distances of its neighbors if a shorter path is found, and record the predecessor node.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the edge relaxation step, which is the heart of Dijkstra's algorithm. For a given node u with distance dist[u], we examine each neighbor v with edge weight w. If dist[u] + w < dist[v], we have found a shorter path to v via u, so we update dist[v] to dist[u] + w and set predecessor[v] = u. The intuition is that we are continuously improving our estimate of the shortest path by considering new routes through recently processed nodes. This works because with non-negative weights, once a node is processed (popped from the min-heap), its distance is final, and we use it to potentially improve others. The step-by-step: for each edge (u,v,w), check if dist[u] + w < dist[v], if true, update dist[v] and pred[v]. Time per relaxation is O(1), but it's called for each edge in the graph. Space is O(1) extra per relaxation. This feeds into the key sub-routine by being the operation performed when processing a node in Dijkstra's algorithm. Common mistakes include not checking the condition correctly (e.g., using <= instead of <, which is okay but may change predecessors), updating predecessors even when distance doesn't improve, and forgetting to handle infinity distances. Real code pattern: if dist[u] + w < dist[v]: dist[v] = dist[u] + w; pred[v] = u.

examples.trace: For edge from u=1 to v=2 with weight 3: dist[u]=5, w=3, dist[v]=inf. Check: 5+3=8 < inf, true. Update dist[2]=8, pred[2]=1. No change to others.

ladders.title: Building Block — Min-Heap Operations for Priority Queue
ladders.desc: Implement and use a min-heap to always extract the node with the smallest tentative distance efficiently.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves using a min-heap (priority queue) to manage nodes in order of increasing tentative distance during Dijkstra's algorithm. The intuition is that Dijkstra requires repeatedly finding the unprocessed node with the smallest distance; a min-heap allows this in O(log n) time per operation, crucial for efficiency. A min-heap is a binary tree where each node's value is less than or equal to its children's values, ensuring the smallest element is at the root. We use it to store (distance, node) pairs, so popping gives the smallest distance node. The step-by-step: initialize heap with (0, source); while heap not empty, pop (dist, u); if dist > distances[u], skip (lazy deletion); else process u and push (new_distance, v) for neighbors when distances updated. Time per push/pop is O(log V), and since we do O(E) pushes, total O(E log V). Space is O(V) for heap storage. This feeds into the core logic by providing the order in which nodes are processed for relaxation. Common mistakes include not using a heap (leading to O(V^2) time), not handling duplicate entries in heap (use lazy deletion), and incorrect heap implementation (e.g., not maintaining heap property). Real code pattern: Use heapq module in Python with heapq.heappush and heapq.heappop.

examples.input: Initial heap: [(0,0)]. Distances: [0, inf, inf]. After relaxing from node 0: edges 0->1 weight 2, 0->2 weight 3.
examples.trace: Start heap: [(0,0)]. Pop (0,0): process node 0. Relax edge to 1: new dist=2, push (2,1) to heap. Relax edge to 2: new dist=3, push (3,2) to heap. Heap now contains (2,1) and (3,2); heap property ensures (2,1) is at root.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Represent a weighted graph using an adjacency list to efficiently access neighbors and edge weights.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves representing a graph in a way that supports efficient traversal for Dijkstra's algorithm. An adjacency list is preferred because it allows O(1) access to the list of neighbors for a node and is space-efficient for sparse graphs. The intuition is that for each node, we store a list of (neighbor, weight) pairs, enabling quick iteration over outgoing edges. This works because graphs in pathfinding problems often have varying degrees, and adjacency lists adapt well. Step-by-step: create a list of lists or dictionary where graph[u] contains pairs (v, w) for each edge from u to v with weight w. For undirected graphs, add both directions. Time to build is O(V + E), space is O(V + E). This feeds into the building block by providing the structure needed for relaxation steps. Common mistakes include using adjacency matrix (O(V^2) space), incorrect indexing, forgetting to handle weights, or not initializing for all nodes. Real code pattern: graph = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for each edge (u,v,w): graph[u].append((v,w)).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Graphs and Nodes
ladders.desc: Learn what a graph is, with nodes and weighted edges, as a prerequisite for pathfinding algorithms.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces graphs, which are fundamental data structures used in Dijkstra's algorithm. A graph consists of nodes (also called vertices) and edges that connect pairs of nodes. In weighted graphs, each edge has a weight (or cost) representing the distance, time, or other metric between nodes. The intuition is that graphs model networks like roads, social connections, or computer networks, where finding shortest paths is a common problem. Nodes are typically represented as integers (0,1,2,...) for simplicity. Edges can be directed (one-way) or undirected (two-way); Dijkstra works on directed graphs with non-negative weights. Understanding this is crucial because Dijkstra operates on this structure to find paths. Step-by-step: visualize a graph with circles as nodes and arrows as edges with weights. Time and space are not analyzed here as it's conceptual. This feeds into the basic operation by providing the raw data that gets represented in an adjacency list. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with edges, misunderstanding weights, or not grasping directionality. Real code pattern: No code yet, but think of nodes as indices in a list.

approaches.name: Bellman-Ford with Path Tracking O(VE)
approaches.summary: Iteratively relax all edges V-1 times to find shortest paths, and use a predecessor array to reconstruct the path. This works with negative edge weights but not negative cycles.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Shortest Path with Path Reconstruction using Bellman-Ford
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Bellman-Ford algorithm with path tracking to find shortest paths and reconstruct the actual path sequence.
ladders.explanation: The Bellman-Ford algorithm solves the single-source shortest path problem by iteratively relaxing all edges V-1 times, where V is the number of vertices. This approach works with graphs that may contain negative edge weights (but not negative cycles reachable from the source). The key insight is that after V-1 iterations, the shortest paths will have been found if no negative cycles exist. We maintain two arrays: dist[] to store shortest distances from source, and prev[] to track predecessors for path reconstruction. The algorithm consists of three phases: 1) Initialization (set source distance to 0, others to infinity), 2) Relaxation (V-1 iterations where we update distances if a shorter path is found), 3) Negative cycle detection (optional check after V-1 iterations). For path reconstruction, we use the prev[] array to backtrack from target to source. The intuition behind V-1 iterations is that in the worst case, a shortest path can have at most V-1 edges (no cycles in shortest paths). Each iteration relaxes all edges, potentially propagating distance updates through the graph. Time complexity is O(VE) where E is edges, space is O(V). Common mistakes include: forgetting to initialize distances properly, incorrect iteration count (should be V-1, not V), not handling disconnected vertices (remain at infinity), and incorrect path reconstruction when no path exists. This connects to the parent problem by providing both distance and actual path information.

examples.input: Graph: 4 vertices (0-3), edges: 0→1 weight 4, 0→2 weight 5, 1→2 weight -2, 1→3 weight 6, 2→3 weight 3. Source: 0, Target: 3
examples.trace:
Initialization: dist = [0, ∞, ∞, ∞], prev = [None, None, None, None]
Iteration 1:
  Edge 0→1: dist[1] = min(∞, 0+4) = 4, prev[1] = 0
  Edge 0→2: dist[2] = min(∞, 0+5) = 5, prev[2] = 0
  Edge 1→2: dist[2] = min(5, 4+(-2)) = 2, prev[2] = 1
  Edge 1→3: dist[3] = min(∞, 4+6) = 10, prev[3] = 1
  Edge 2→3: dist[3] = min(10, 2+3) = 5, prev[3] = 2
Iteration 2:
  Edge 0→1: dist[1] = 4 (no change)
  Edge 0→2: dist[2] = 2 (no change)
  Edge 1→2: dist[2] = 2 (no change)
  Edge 1→3: dist[3] = min(5, 4+6) = 5 (no change)
  Edge 2→3: dist[3] = min(5, 2+3) = 5 (no change)
Iteration 3 (V-1=3):
  No changes (converged)
Path reconstruction from 3: prev[3]=2, prev[2]=1, prev[1]=0 → path [0,1,2,3]
Final: distance=5, path=[0,1,2,3]

Iteration 1:

  Edge 1→2: dist[2] = min(0, 0+(-1)) = -1, prev[2] = 1

Negative cycle detected (distances keep decreasing)
For this example, assuming no negative cycle reachable from source (edge 2→0 might not be in path to 2)
If we ignore 2→0 edge (directed away from path):
After iteration 1: dist[2]=0, path [0,1,2]
Final: distance=0, path=[0,1,2]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Edge Relaxation with Predecessor Tracking
ladders.desc: The core relaxation operation that updates shortest distances and tracks predecessors when a shorter path is found.
ladders.explanation: Edge relaxation is the fundamental operation in Bellman-Ford that potentially improves the current shortest distance estimate. Given an edge from u to v with weight w, we check if dist[u] + w < dist[v]. If true, we've found a shorter path to v through u, so we update dist[v] = dist[u] + w and set prev[v] = u. The intuition is simple: if going from source to u takes dist[u] and then taking edge u→v adds w, this new path (dist[u] + w) might be shorter than the current best path to v (dist[v]). This operation propagates distance improvements through the graph. In pseudocode: if dist[u] + w < dist[v]: dist[v] = dist[u] + w; prev[v] = u. Time complexity per relaxation is O(1). Space is O(1) plus the maintained arrays. This connects to the full algorithm as it's the operation repeated in the main loop. Common mistakes include: using > instead of <, forgetting to update predecessor, relaxing edges in wrong order (order doesn't matter in Bellman-Ford but affects convergence speed), and integer overflow with large weights. The predecessor update is crucial for path reconstruction - it creates a reverse linked list from target back to source.

examples.input: Current: dist[0]=0, dist[1]=5, dist[2]=∞. prev[1]=0. Edge: 1→2 weight 3
examples.trace:
Check: dist[1] + 3 = 5 + 3 = 8
Compare with current dist[2] = ∞
8 < ∞ is true
Update dist[2] = 8
Update prev[2] = 1 (since we reached 2 through 1)

examples.input: Current: dist[0]=0, dist[1]=4, dist[2]=6. prev[1]=0, prev[2]=0. Edge: 1→2 weight 1
examples.trace:
Check: dist[1] + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5
Compare with current dist[2] = 6
5 < 6 is true
Update dist[2] = 5
Update prev[2] = 1 (shorter path through 1)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Relaxation for V-1 Passes
ladders.desc: The main loop structure that applies edge relaxation repeatedly to propagate shortest path distances.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of Bellman-Ford is repeating edge relaxation V-1 times, where V is the number of vertices. This works because in the worst case, the shortest path from source to any vertex can have at most V-1 edges (if there were V edges, there would be a cycle, which couldn't be part of a shortest path unless negative cycles exist). Each pass through all edges allows distance updates to propagate one more edge away from the source. After i passes, the algorithm has found all shortest paths with at most i edges. After V-1 passes, it has considered all possible simple paths. The pseudocode: for i in range(V-1): for each edge (u,v,w): relax(u,v,w). Time complexity is O(VE) since we do V-1 passes over E edges. Space is O(V) for distance and predecessor arrays. This connects to the full algorithm as the main computational loop. Common mistakes include: doing V passes instead of V-1 (wasteful but correct), stopping early when no updates occur (optimization but not required), incorrect edge iteration order affecting correctness (order doesn't matter for correctness but affects which paths are found when). The intuition is like spreading ripples in water - each pass lets the 'shortest distance wave' travel one more edge outward from the source.

examples.input: Graph: 3 vertices, edges: 0→1:2, 1→2:3, 0→2:6. V=3, so V-1=2 passes
examples.output: After pass 1: dist=[0,2,5], After pass 2: dist=[0,2,5] (converged)
examples.trace:
Initial: dist=[0,∞,∞]
Pass 1:
  Edge 0→1: relax → dist[1]=2
  Edge 1→2: relax → dist[2]=5
  Edge 0→2: relax → dist[2]=min(5,6)=5
Pass 2:
  Edge 0→1: no change (2)
  Edge 1→2: no change (5)
  Edge 0→2: no change (5)
Converged after 1 pass, but algorithm does 2 passes

examples.input: Graph: 4 vertices, edges: 0→1:1, 1→2:1, 2→3:1. V=4, so V-1=3 passes
examples.output: Pass1: dist[3]=∞, Pass2: dist[3]=3, Pass3: no change

Initial: dist=[0,∞,∞,∞]
Pass 1:
  0→1: dist[1]=1
  1→2: dist[2]=∞ (can't update yet)
  2→3: dist[3]=∞
Pass 2:
  0→1: dist[1]=1
  1→2: dist[2]=2
  2→3: dist[3]=∞
Pass 3:
  0→1: dist[1]=1
  1→2: dist[2]=2
  2→3: dist[3]=3
Shows how updates propagate one edge per pass

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation with Edge List
ladders.desc: Representing a weighted directed graph as a list of edges for Bellman-Ford iteration.
ladders.explanation: Bellman-Ford requires iterating over all edges repeatedly, so an edge list representation is ideal. Each edge is stored as a triple (u, v, w) representing source vertex u, destination vertex v, and weight w. This is simpler than adjacency lists or matrices for Bellman-Ford because we just need to iterate all edges without concern for vertex adjacency. The edge list can be stored as an array of tuples or a list of objects. Time to iterate all edges is O(E). Space is O(E). This connects to the core logic as it provides the edges to relax. Common mistakes include: forgetting direction (Bellman-Ford works on directed edges; for undirected, represent as two directed edges), incorrect weight storage (negative weights allowed), and including self-loops (edges from vertex to itself - allowed but usually not useful for shortest paths). The intuition is that we need to consider every possible 'step' in the graph during relaxation, and the edge list gives us exactly that.

examples.input: Graph with 3 vertices: edges from 0→1 weight 2, 1→2 weight 3, 0→2 weight 5
examples.trace:
Vertex 0 to 1 with weight 2 → (0,1,2)
Vertex 1 to 2 with weight 3 → (1,2,3)
Vertex 0 to 2 with weight 5 → (0,2,5)
Total 3 edges in list
Order doesn't matter for correctness

examples.input: Undirected graph with 2 vertices: edge between 0 and 1 with weight 4
examples.trace:
For undirected graphs, we need to represent as two directed edges
0→1 weight 4 → (0,1,4)
1→0 weight 4 → (1,0,4)
Now Bellman-Ford can traverse in both directions

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Distance Comparison and Update
ladders.desc: The fundamental comparison and assignment operation at the heart of relaxation.
ladders.explanation: This is the atomic operation: comparing a candidate distance (current distance to u plus edge weight w) with the current best distance to v, and updating if the candidate is smaller. Mathematically: if dist[u] + w < dist[v], then set dist[v] = dist[u] + w. This is essentially finding the minimum of two values. The intuition is that we're always keeping the smallest distance found so far. In programming terms, it's a simple if-statement with assignment. Time O(1), space O(1). This connects to edge relaxation as the core conditional check. Common mistakes include: using <= instead of < (works but may unnecessary updates), integer overflow when adding large numbers (use appropriate data types), and comparing infinity values (need special handling - infinity plus anything is still infinity). This operation embodies the greedy aspect of shortest path algorithms: always take the shorter option when found.

examples.input: dist[u]=5, dist[v]=10, w=3
examples.trace:
Calculate candidate: 5 + 3 = 8
Compare: 8 < 10 is true
So update dist[v] from 10 to 8

examples.input: dist[u]=7, dist[v]=4, w=2
examples.trace:
Calculate candidate: 7 + 2 = 9
Compare: 9 < 4 is false
So dist[v] remains 4

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Vertices and Edges
ladders.desc: Understanding the basic components of a graph: vertices (nodes) and directed weighted edges.
ladders.explanation: A graph consists of vertices (also called nodes) and edges connecting them. In shortest path problems, we use directed weighted graphs: each edge has a direction (from source vertex to destination vertex) and a weight (cost to traverse that edge). Vertices are typically numbered 0 to V-1. An edge is represented as (u, v, w) meaning from vertex u to vertex v with weight w. Weights can be positive, negative, or zero. The intuition is that vertices are locations and edges are roads with travel costs. This is the foundation because Bellman-Ford operates on these basic elements. Time to understand a graph is O(V+E), space to store is O(V+E). This connects to graph representation as we need to store these elements. Common mistakes include: confusing undirected vs directed edges, misunderstanding zero-based vs one-based indexing, and not handling negative weights correctly (they're allowed in Bellman-Ford). For beginners: think of vertices as cities on a map, edges as one-way roads with toll costs (weights).

approaches.name: A* with Path Reconstruction O(b^d)
approaches.summary: Extend Dijkstra by incorporating a heuristic to guide the search towards the destination, using a predecessor map to rebuild the path. This is optimal with an admissible heuristic for faster convergence in many cases.

ladders.title: Full Problem — A* with Path Reconstruction for Shortest Path
ladders.desc: Implement A* algorithm with path reconstruction to find the shortest path between two nodes in a weighted graph.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution using A* algorithm with path reconstruction. The intuition is to extend Dijkstra's algorithm by incorporating a heuristic function that estimates the distance from each node to the destination, guiding the search more efficiently towards the goal. This approach is optimal when the heuristic is admissible (never overestimates the true cost). The algorithm maintains a priority queue of nodes to explore, ordered by f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where g(n) is the cost from the start to node n, and h(n) is the heuristic estimate from n to the destination. A predecessor map tracks the path. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize g-scores (start=0, others=∞), f-scores (start=h(start)), predecessor map, and priority queue with start node. 2. While queue not empty: pop node with lowest f-score. If it's the destination, reconstruct path using predecessors and return. Otherwise, for each neighbor: calculate tentative g = g(current) + edge weight. If tentative g < g(neighbor), update g(neighbor), f(neighbor) = g(neighbor) + h(neighbor), set predecessor, and add neighbor to queue. 3. If queue empties without finding destination, return no path. Time complexity is O(b^d) where b is branching factor and d is depth, but typically faster than Dijkstra with a good heuristic. Space is O(V) for storing g-scores and predecessors. Connection to L1: This uses the path reconstruction subroutine from L1. Common mistakes: using an inadmissible heuristic (breaks optimality), not updating f-scores properly, or forgetting to handle unreachable nodes. Real code pattern: Use heapq for priority queue, dictionaries for g-scores and predecessors, and a heuristic function like Euclidean or Manhattan distance for grid graphs.

examples.input: Graph: nodes A,B,C,D; edges: A-B=1, A-C=4, B-C=2, B-D=5, C-D=1; start=A, end=D; heuristic h(n)=straight-line distance (A:3, B:2, C:1, D:0)
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize g(A)=0, g others=∞, f(A)=0+3=3, queue=[(3,A)], pred={}. Step 2: Pop A, f=3. Neighbors: B (tentative g=0+1=1 < ∞, update g(B)=1, f(B)=1+2=3, pred[B]=A, queue=[(3,B)]), C (tentative g=0+4=4 < ∞, update g(C)=4, f(C)=4+1=5, pred[C]=A, queue=[(3,B),(5,C)]). Step 3: Pop B, f=3. Neighbors: C (tentative g=1+2=3 < 4, update g(C)=3, f(C)=3+1=4, pred[C]=B, queue=[(4,C),(5,C old removed)]), D (tentative g=1+5=6 < ∞, update g(D)=6, f(D)=6+0=6, pred[D]=B, queue=[(4,C),(6,D)]). Step 4: Pop C, f=4. Neighbors: D (tentative g=3+1=4 < 6, update g(D)=4, f(D)=4+0=4, pred[D]=C, queue=[(4,D),(6,D old removed)]). Step 5: Pop D, f=4, is destination. Reconstruct path: D<-C<-B<-A gives A->B->C->D, cost g(D)=4.

examples.input: Graph: nodes X,Y,Z; edges: X-Y=2, Y-Z=3; start=X, end=Z; heuristic h(n)=0 for all (degenerates to Dijkstra)

Step 3: Pop Y, f=2. Neighbor Z: tentative g=2+3=5 < ∞, update g(Z)=5, f(Z)=5+0=5, pred[Z]=Y, queue=[(5,Z)]. Step 4: Pop Z, f=5, is destination. Reconstruct: Z<-Y<-X gives X->Y->Z, cost 5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Path Reconstruction from Predecessor Map
ladders.desc: Reconstruct the shortest path from start to destination using a predecessor map built during search.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine reconstructs the path once the destination is found. The intuition is that during A* search, we record the predecessor of each node (the node we came from to reach it with the best known cost). This forms a chain from destination back to start. To get the path in forward order, we reverse this chain. Step-by-step: 1. Start with an empty list for the path. 2. Set current node = destination. 3. While current node is not None: insert current node at beginning of path list, set current node = predecessor[current]. 4. If start node is not at beginning of path (i.e., chain broken), return error or no path. Time complexity is O(L) where L is path length, as we traverse the chain once. Space is O(L) for storing the path. Connection to L0: This is called after A* finds the destination to output the path. Common mistakes: not handling cycles (shouldn't occur with proper A*), forgetting to reverse, or assuming predecessor map includes start (it shouldn't, as start has no predecessor). Real code pattern: Use a while loop to backtrack from destination to start, building a list, then reverse it or build it in reverse order.

examples.input: predecessor map: {B: A, C: B, D: C}, start=A, end=D
examples.trace: Step 1: path=[]. Step 2: current=D. Step 3: D not None, path=[D], current=pred[D]=C. Step 4: C not None, path=[C,D], current=pred[C]=B. Step 5: B not None, path=[B,C,D], current=pred[B]=A. Step 6: A not None, path=[A,B,C,D], current=pred[A]=None (or not in map). Step 7: current=None, stop. Path is A->B->C->D.

examples.trace: Step 1: path=[]. Step 2: current=Z. Step 3: Z not None, path=[Z], current=pred[Z]=Y. Step 4: Y not None, path=[Y,Z], current=pred[Y]=X. Step 5: X not None, path=[X,Y,Z], current=pred[X]=None. Step 6: stop. Path X->Y->Z.

ladders.title: Core Logic — A* Search with Heuristic-Guided Priority Queue
ladders.desc: Implement the A* search algorithm using a priority queue ordered by f(n)=g(n)+h(n) to explore nodes.
ladders.explanation: This is the core logic of A* search. The intuition is to prioritize nodes that are estimated to be on the shortest path by combining the actual cost from start (g(n)) and a heuristic estimate to the destination (h(n)). This guides the search more directly towards the goal compared to Dijkstra's uniform cost search. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize g-scores: start=0, others=∞. 2. Initialize priority queue with start node, keyed by f(start)=g(start)+h(start). 3. While queue not empty: pop node with smallest f-score. If node is destination, stop. Else, for each neighbor: calculate tentative g = g(current) + edge weight. If tentative g < g(neighbor), update g(neighbor), calculate f(neighbor)=g(neighbor)+h(neighbor), and push neighbor to queue (or update if already in queue). 4. If queue empties, destination unreachable. Time complexity depends on heuristic; in worst case O(b^d). Space is O(V) for g-scores and queue. Connection to L0: This is the main search loop without path reconstruction. Common mistakes: not using admissible heuristic (must satisfy h(n) ≤ true cost to destination), incorrect priority queue updates, or not revisiting nodes when a better path is found. Real code pattern: Use heapq in Python, with a dictionary for g-scores, and a heuristic function.

examples.input: Graph: nodes P,Q,R; edges P-Q=3, Q-R=2, P-R=6; start=P, end=R; h(P)=5, h(Q)=2, h(R)=0
examples.trace: Step 1: g(P)=0, g others=∞, f(P)=0+5=5, queue=[(5,P)]. Step 2: Pop P, f=5. Neighbors: Q (tentative g=0+3=3 < ∞, update g(Q)=3, f(Q)=3+2=5, queue=[(5,Q)]), R (tentative g=0+6=6 < ∞, update g(R)=6, f(R)=6+0=6, queue=[(5,Q),(6,R)]). Step 3: Pop Q, f=5. Neighbors: R (tentative g=3+2=5 < 6, update g(R)=5, f(R)=5+0=5, queue=[(5,R),(6,R old)]). Step 4: Pop R, f=5, is destination, stop with g(R)=5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Heuristic Function Design for Graph Search
ladders.desc: Design and implement an admissible heuristic function that estimates cost to destination for use in A*.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating a heuristic function h(n) that estimates the cost from node n to the destination. The intuition is that a good heuristic guides the search efficiently while ensuring optimality if it is admissible (never overestimates the true cost). Common heuristics include Euclidean distance for spatial graphs or Manhattan distance for grid graphs. Step-by-step: 1. Define the heuristic based on problem constraints (e.g., if graph represents coordinates, use distance formula). 2. Ensure admissibility: for all nodes n, h(n) ≤ true shortest cost from n to destination. 3. Implement as a function that takes a node and returns a non-negative estimate. Time complexity is O(1) per call if precomputed or simple calculation. Space is O(1) or O(V) if storing values. Connection to L2: This heuristic is used in the f(n) calculation during A* search. Common mistakes: using an inadmissible heuristic (breaks A* optimality), over-complicating it, or not handling non-spatial graphs (may use zero heuristic, degenerating to Dijkstra). Real code pattern: For grid with coordinates (x,y), h(n) = |x_n - x_dest| + |y_n - y_dest| for Manhattan, or sqrt((x_n-x_dest)^2 + (y_n-y_dest)^2) for Euclidean.

examples.input: Nodes with coordinates: A(0,0), B(2,0), C(2,2), D(0,2); destination=D; use Manhattan distance
examples.trace: Manhattan distance: |x1-x2| + |y1-y2|. For A(0,0) to D(0,2): |0-0|+|0-2|=0+2=2. For B(2,0) to D(0,2): |2-0|+|0-2|=2+2=4, but wait, recalc: |2-0|+|0-2|=2+2=4? Check: B to D is (2,0) to (0,2), so |2-0|=2, |0-2|=2, sum=4. But in example, h(B)=2 is given, so assume coordinates: A(0,0), B(1,0), C(1,1), D(0,1) for simplicity. Revised: A(0,0) to D(0,1): |0-0|+|0-1|=0+1=1, but need h(A)=2, so adjust: use D(0,2). Let's use consistent: A(0,0), B(2,0), C(2,2), D(0,2). Then h(A)=|0-0|+|0-2|=0+2=2, h(B)=|2-0|+|0-2|=2+2=4, h(C)=|2-0|+|2-2|=2+0=2, h(D)=0. To match output, assume different: A(0,0), B(1,0), C(2,0), D(2,2). Then h(A)=|0-2|+|0-2|=2+2=4, not match. Let's stick with given output as example: h(A)=2, h(B)=2, h(C)=0, h(D)=0 implies coordinates like A(0,0), B(1,1), C(2,2), D(2,2)? Actually, for h(C)=0, C must be at destination, so C=D. So example is abstract; we'll compute: For node A, estimate 2; B, estimate 2; C, estimate 0; D, estimate 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Priority Queue Management for Node Exploration
ladders.desc: Implement operations to insert, update, and pop nodes from a min-priority queue keyed by f-scores.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves managing a priority queue to always explore the node with the smallest f-score first. The intuition is that a priority queue efficiently retrieves the minimum element, which is crucial for A*'s greedy-like search. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an empty priority queue (e.g., using a heap). 2. Insert: push a (f-score, node) tuple onto the heap. 3. Pop: remove and return the node with the smallest f-score. 4. Update: if a node's f-score improves, we need to update its position in the queue; since standard heaps don't support decrease-key efficiently, a common approach is to push a new entry and ignore stale ones when popping. Time complexity: O(log N) for insert and pop, where N is queue size. Space is O(N). Connection to L2: This queue is used in the A* search loop to select the next node to explore. Common mistakes: not handling duplicate entries (can push new entries and check g-scores when popping), using wrong priority order, or not using a stable tie-breaker. Real code pattern: In Python, use heapq module with tuples (f, node), and when popping, check if the node's g-score matches the current best to skip stale entries.

examples.input: Initial queue: [(5,A), (3,B), (7,C)]. Pop, then insert (4,D), then pop.
examples.output: First pop: B, second pop: D
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue is min-heap: [(3,B), (5,A), (7,C)]. Pop: removes B (smallest f=3). Step 2: Insert (4,D): heap becomes [(4,D), (5,A), (7,C)]. Step 3: Pop: removes D (smallest f=4).

examples.input: Queue: [(10,X), (5,Y)]. Update Y to f=3 by pushing new entry, then pop twice.
examples.output: First pop: Y (f=3), second pop: X
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial heap: [(5,Y), (10,X)]. Step 2: Update Y by pushing (3,Y): heap becomes [(3,Y), (10,X), (5,Y stale)]. Step 3: Pop: removes Y with f=3 (new entry). Step 4: Pop: next smallest is X with f=10 (ignoring stale Y with f=5 if checked).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation and Neighbor Traversal
ladders.desc: Represent a weighted graph and traverse neighbors of a node, a prerequisite for pathfinding algorithms.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept of representing a graph and accessing its neighbors. The intuition is that graphs model connections between nodes, and algorithms like A* need to explore these connections. A common representation is an adjacency list: for each node, store a list of (neighbor, weight) pairs. Step-by-step: 1. Define the graph structure: e.g., dictionary mapping node to list of tuples. 2. To traverse neighbors of a node: look up the node in the adjacency list and iterate over the list. 3. Handle cases like missing nodes (no neighbors) or directed vs. undirected graphs (store edges accordingly). Time complexity to get neighbors is O(degree of node). Space is O(V + E) for storing the graph. Connection to L4: This graph is used when exploring neighbors during priority queue operations. Common mistakes: incorrect edge weights, forgetting to handle both directions in undirected graphs, or using inefficient representations like adjacency matrix for sparse graphs. Real code pattern: In Python, graph = {'A': [('B', 1), ('C', 4)], 'B': [('A', 1), ('C', 2)]} for an undirected weighted graph.

examples.input: Graph: nodes A,B,C; edges A-B=2, A-C=3, B-C=1 (undirected). Traverse neighbors of A.
examples.trace: Step 1: Represent graph: {'A': [('B',2), ('C',3)], 'B': [('A',2), ('C',1)], 'C': [('A',3), ('B',1)]}. Step 2: For node A, look up graph['A'] = [('B',2), ('C',3)]. Step 3: Iterate: first neighbor B with weight 2, second neighbor C with weight 3.
